video_title: Passive Income on Ethereum for All: How Rocket Pool Scales Liquid Staking
transcript: I I won't I won't pick on Kil too much, >> but the the Kilm issue where they had compromised keys and had to exit and come back in again. I mean, I kind of feel from that's that's definitely something that, you know, will happen, but those sorts of risks don't happen with Rocket Ball because we're so decentralized. So, we we have almost 2,000 node operators in 90 different countries. each one's different and you know there there is no there is no way that you can have that issue where you have to exit all of your pollinators and and so that that type of issue doesn't really exist with rocket ball those sort of centralization >> definitely >> all right so thrilled to have Darren Langley or Langers from RocketPool here on the defiant rocket obviously is one of the largest Ethereum liquid staking platforms and they just had one of the their most major upgrades the um the Saturn upgrade. So, Langanger's welcome and for those in my audience who might not be so familiar with RocketPool, can you explain uh what it does and what sets you apart from other Ethereum staking protocols? >> Yeah, yeah, I'd love to. So, RocketPool is a decentralized liquid staking protocol. So we kind of have two sides. On one side we have liquid stakers who deposit ETH into the protocol and earn beacon chain staking rewards on their on their ETH. And then on the other side we have node operators who uh deposit essentially a bond for becoming a node operator. Um anyone can be a node operator with blockable. I'll get to that in a sec. But node operators uh deposit a bond and they we match them up basically with the liquid staking ETH. The node operator stakes the ETH on behalf of liquid stakers and for that they also earn uh like an extra um commission um on their ETH staking. What makes Rocket Pool special I guess is that everything's permissionless. Uh well there's there's probably there's layers. We're like an onion but uh the f the first thing is that it's permissionless. Anyone can be a node operator. Um anyone can deposit into Arith and we have lots of integrations and that kind of build on a as well. And then the other aspect is that because we're so our not because we're permission permissionless our not payers are decentralized and we've kind of the probably one of the only staking protocols that have been built in a Ethereum aligned way from the very beginning and so we also have decentralized governance. So we're kind of like decentralized throughout the stack which is which is what makes Rocket Pool special. >> So how does the fact that you're decentralized affect or impact the end user? like what how does it benefit the end user? >> Yeah, that's a great question. So, so with Ari um holders key thing around the design with because we're a a smart contract protocol, no there's no middleman involved uh and no party has to really trust each other with um uh with custody of the ETH. So a an R E holder deposits their ETH and it's staked by a node operator but the protocol ensures that the protocol rules are followed and so each party gets what they uh what they're supposed to and so that's that's a that's a big aspect is kind of the trust minim minimization aspects of it. The the other part is I mean from a governance perspective as a node operator you you stake RPL which is or you can stake RPL as our token which is our token and so you can actually become part of the governance structure for market pool. So uh so you have a say in the direction of the protocol and how upgrades are performed and all of that sort of thing. >> Awesome. Okay. So speaking of upgrades um can you talk about Saturn one this um this major upgrade you guys just did? >> Yeah. Yeah. So, so Saturn is actually like a precursor to Saturn called Saturn Zero. Um, which I won't go into too too much, but the Saturn 1 program has a couple of different topline goals. The first thing is to scale the protocol. So, actually one of the important aspects of being a decentralized protocol is the bond that the node operators put up and current before Saturn it was 8 E per validator which is a is a bit of a ceiling for our scaling. So because we rely on our node operators to deposit that 8 E, we obviously have to have enough node operators to do that. Um and for quite a while we were supply bound because we had such a huge demand in RT that the note operators we didn't have enough node operators essentially. So a big thing it was for us to go from 8 E to 4 E which 4 bonds which sounds quite easy. It might be just a number change, but under the hood, it's a bit more complicated than that. Um, to make sure that we give our ETH holders the protection that they really need and expect from Ari as a solid asset, we we had to kind of make some structural changes around the protocol. Uh, I I can say that Saturn is really rocket v2. Uh, there's not really a part of RocketPool that hasn't been touched by the by the upgrade. the the other so this the top line items are scaling some stuff around the RPL tokconomics and then there's essentially withdrawal liquidity so withdrawal liquidity for our ETH holders what it does is the protocol just prioritizes withdrawal withdrawal liquidity much more and so that way that's actually a lot better for kind of more conservative um holders particularly the the very large players because they obviously want some guarantees in terms of being able to withdraw instantly and that's what this this withdrawal liquidity changes. >> Um okay so uh summarizing biggest changes are around scaling the number of node operators by lowering the amount of ETH that's required to four ETH from eight ETH. So that should allow for a larger number of node operators which in turn should also allow for more R E to be issued. Right. >> Yeah. a lot more a lot more RAS >> and then and then the other aspect is our ETH liquidity. So the ability to not only mint mint more R E with with increasing amount of node operators but also to redeem ETH for our ETH um how how are you achieving uh this increased liquidity? >> Yeah. So it's really a change of priority. So currently we have anytime a node operator exits or um uh or rewards come back that's actually put into what we call the withdrawal liquidity buffer and and so that that kind of builds up before Saturn new deposits into the protocol by our ETH holders would actually go to spinning up validators but now what we've done is we've changed it so that that actually goes to the withdrawal buffer first. So there's a threshold of the amount of withdrawal liquidity that we require. Um and so if it's under that threshold, then everything goes to making sure that we meet that threshold. Once it tips over that, which it currently is of as of today, um the flows into spinning up validators and growing. Essentially what we're doing is we're prioritizing the withdrawal liquidity over growth because the withdrawal liquidity is gives better guarantees for holders and it also has another effect in the sense that it stabilizes the R price because more withdrawal liquidity means that there's more arbing of the the price against the market >> and so you end up with a a much more stable price. >> Right. Right. Yeah. I was going to I was going to ask that whether you know um our ETH is expected to keep the peg to ETH better because of this increased liquidity and yeah it sounds like that's that's one of the implications of it. So the point of having our ETH this liquid staking token is obviously to use it across DeFi. So right now what like what's the main use case like what what are people doing with RE? >> Yeah. So will be there are a couple of um different things. There is probably the the most thing is borrowing against it. So you can put it into things like spark and Ave. So you can have an ETH position essentially you swap it into um RT which earn staking rewards and then you put it into Ave and then you borrow labels or or or something against it so that you can use that unlock that capital for use in some way. So that's like kind of the one of the the first use cases. The other use case is kind of expanding on that. Actually I should say that I would say most people just swap into Ari and then just leave it. That is that is definitely a valid use case. But in terms of itself borrowing against the is is one thing. The extension to that is essentially leverage. So there is a like a looping strategy where you can earn more yield. Obviously, with leverage comes risk, and so that's the that's the trade-off there, but there's there's definitely I think they call it hyperstaking where essentially you leverage um the the ETH against ETH and you kind of like do it a number of times. So, that's one thing. Um I'm not a massive DeFi guy, but we we actually have a partner called Rock Solid who have a a vault, and that's the other that's kind of a way of abstracting the complexity away. So people deposit it into a vault and then you have a manager that manages that on the DeFi side. So they actually choose the strategies. They're very transparent about what the strategies are and they kind of give you updates on what the strategies are, but they can put it into the different strategies and earn the yield on that. Whether that's providing liquidity as well is another is another use case. Putting it into something like balancer or unis swap to earn trading fees is another thing. So yeah, there's there's quite a few different use cases and there's more and there's more and more. One thing that makes a liquid staking token pretty exciting is that it is a primitive on which to build up of things because it is a productive asset. It's a yield bearing asset and so with that comes uh lots of ways that you can use that yield or uh the asset itself. >> Can you explain how yield occurs to our ETH? Does the token itself increase in in value according to the yield that you've gained or is does the yield go to um an Ethereum address? Like how are ETH holders able to actually earn that yield? >> Yeah, so it's actually pretty simple. Um when you swap ETH for our ETH, um there are kind of like two ways that a value acrewing token can be built. There's a one called a rebasing token and a non-rebasing token. The rebasing token is where the value stays basically um the same as the the underlying asset but the quantity goes up and a non-rebasing token is where the um the quantity stays the same but the the value against the underlying goes up. So, US is a non-rebasing token. What that really means for kind of like the experience is that you deposit ETH, you get our ETH back, you hold our ETH for a certain amount of time, and when you swap our ETH back to ETH, you get more than we put in. That's essentially the flow. And that's because that, you know, difference in yield is essentially the uh the rewards are being generated from that ETH. Um, so really represents your principle, your deposit plus the rewards that it is generated. >> Got it. And then for the node operators themselves, does does the yield calculations or mechanics change at all with Saturn one? >> Uh, it it does. Node operator actually um earns earns more uh in Saturn than than they do in um this there's two concepts in rocket pool. There's these mini pools and now mega pools. So, uh, minors earn like a a 14% commission. It's a bit more complicated than that because there's there's a few different types of minip, but there in general they earn 14% commission. Sorry, if they're staking RPL, they earn a 14% commission. If they don't, then it's a 5% commission, but it's it's 8 ETH. Um, and then um uh then there is uh the the rest is from liquid stakers. And so, uh, with node operators, what they do is they earn, um, a mission on the rewards they generate on behalf of the liquid stakers. Uh, so the the rest of the ETH that we provide them. Um, that's the 8 ETH. Uh, that's the thing that they're earning the commission on. With Saturn, because it's a four ETH, they're earning a commission on a much larger part of the pie. So that we actually reduce the commission to um 5% um in Saturn, but they will still be earning more than they would be even if they had a 14% um in the old system. So it's just because the pie is bigger from the liquid staking side. And there's some good stuff on our website that kind of explain that, but essentially um they they earn a greater commission and they definitely earn a greater commission than kind of like solo staking, which is which you you wouldn't obviously get commission on. you earn the you earn the rewards on your bond so the four ETH and plus the commission on the the the liquid sake side. >> Okay. So we've talked a lot about the you know our ETH and note operators but what about uh the rocket pool token itself like the governance token right now I think there's a lot of discussion and um a lot of change on how different protocols are managing their governance tokens. So yeah, we would love to dive into how uh Saturn changes um governance um and and maybe kind of the connection between rocket pool and our RPL RPL. >> It's it's definitely it's definitely a a major aspect of Saturn. So one of the big things so kind of a very brief history of the role of RPL in Rocket Ball. So when we first launched you had to deposit RPL with the ETH your as a node operator. So it's always really been on the node operator side. Um there's nothing really on the E side. R E is is just ETH and and RH. On the node operator side you had to deposit um a minimum of of 10% um uh of the value of RPL with the with the ETH that you're depositing. So uh essentially essentially we were locking up this this RPL and RPL um inflated by 5% per year and 70% of that that inflation went back to node operators um to uh kind of reward them for staking their RPL and give them some extra yield. The there are the kind of some issues with that approach. So Saturn really addresses that and and makes makes RPL much more attractive. So with Saturn Zero, actually Saturn Zero made RPL optional. So as I said before, node operators could stake just ETH if they wanted to with Saturn Zero and they but they could boost their yield by um or boost their commission, sorry, they could boost their commission by staking RPL. With Saturn 1, that changes again, but in a it's it's kind of in a similar vein. So with Saturn 1, what actually happens is you again RPL is is optional. You don't have to take RPL if you don't want to. um it um RPL retains the governance structure that it has being able to participate in governance as it has all the way through. But what actually happens is a percentage of the rewards or the commission the percentage of the commission that are eth holders pay actually goes as ETH goes into a pool and then that pool is then distributed amongst seeking node operators. So what this really does, it provides a much more direct relationship between the success of Rocketpool and the value occurring to the RPL token. Um, and I mean really it should be kind of the the way that you appraise the RPL value as well, but it provides a very direct relationship between the two because the more ETH that the protocol is staking, the more ETH is going to appeal staking notice. essentially the higher the yield on on RPL and the the difference there is that eventually we're going to remove the RPL inflation. So inflation is 5% at the moment that's going to come down to probably 2 and a half to 1.5%. U so that makes RPL much more attractive because it is then a an ETH acrewing token and it has a low low inflation rate. It's really exciting for us. So with RPL now you are able to to earn more ETH rather than have more RPL in inflation and that and the ETH that is distributed to RPL holders correlates directly with the amount of ETH that is being staked on the rocket pool protocol. >> Yeah, that's right. M >> but yeah essentially as we as we grow as a as a protocol if um uh you know that the percentage obviously grows in in number and so uh we end up with more ETH going to RPL holders. Another thing is actually Saturn brings with it another benefit and that's we can actually currently rocket struggles to change the commission rates because just the way it's because of the way it's structured with Saturn that changes because it essentially brings in a universal commission rate and so it means that the um you know governance can actually change the commission over time to be more competitive. So we can adjust the supply side, we can adjust the demand side through the through the commission. So that's that's definitely one thing to make us more nimble um much more able to meet the competition when things change when market conditions change. So that that's definitely one thing. I guess from our point of view, the vision is to be as competitive as possible with those centralized players while still having keeping the uh you know the Ethereum ethos alive which is which is very difficult for us because being a decentralized protocol is much harder than being a centralized player I would argue because we have a lot that we need to consider to be to say permissionless. So yeah, so that that so we're doing putting a lot of work in the background to make sure that we c can compete with the the scaling improvements, draw improvements, um and the flexibility in the commission rates. I think we're in a much better um a much better position than we were previously. >> Awesome. Although I I should say that decentralized is good for business. I think I think Danny Ryan has said this before. And so yeah, I think there's definitely a case to make for Rocket Pool to attract kind of that institutional capital uh that's coming. So I I really hope that's the case. I think the big drivers are the the kind of like the treasury the digital asset treasury companies and staking ETFs that are being coming online at the moment. So they're they're two driving forces that particularly interested in staking uh the staking landscape. Well, actually probably before that there's also the fact that the SEC have clarified the regulation staking staking but also liquid staking as well which was amazing for us and that came probably quicker than I expected which is which is really good to see those those two regulatory aspects. So there's good regulatory wins in terms of the uh that that being cleared up and then you've got these the demand side which is the the the treasury companies and staking ETFs kind of like driving into it as well. I I do think that yeah the queue is very long at the moment. I do think that could have been handled a little bit better but I mean from our perspective uh yeah a big queue is kind of a double-edged sword. you know, it makes it more difficult to get our validators online, but it also makes our ETH more attractive. Um because uh if you are if you're trying to get into um staking and you're just doing it as a through kind of uh normal node staking and it take you currently it's uh 60 days um to to stake whereas with you can deposit and be earning rewards you know instantly. So is a bit of a double-edged sword. You know, there's definitely things that we need to manage from a protocol perspective for having that long queue, but it does make R more attractive in the short term. Um, so there there's definitely those uh those drivers. Yeah. And I think staking staking's always been popular mainly because is a very is kind of that base rate yield. A lot of DeFi there's there's more risk involved. Staking although it's not risk, it's not risk- free. It's not the risk-free rate necessarily, but it's definitely very low risk. So, so there's uh so there's there's that to it as well. >> Yeah, I think well definitely yeah, regulatory clarity has to help of course and greater players wanting to stake or like I should say players wanting to st and also yeah I think right now people are wanting to take risk off. So yeah, maybe having kind of the the lower risk uh yield makes sense. Okay. Now, looking forward and in terms of timeline for the Saturn one, is everything kind of already implemented or is it kind of going to start working in stages? And so yeah, so that's definitely so it was delivered yesterday and it's uh yeah, and it's it's it's working um as it is at the moment. Obviously, the queue is slowing things down a little bit um but we'll get those validators um online. Uh yeah. And so yeah, but it seems to be seems to be working um working really well at the moment. >> Cool. And then going forward, what's next? So we have a very very small core team and a very large DAO. And so Saturn one is actually the culmination of a lot of co-development work between the core team and the DAO. Exaggerating when I say that probably 90% of Saturn one from like the research and and product perspective was the DAO. Um we have a very strong contingent of researchers within the DAO who have have kind of gone through and figured out what we needed as a protocol and then obviously the the governance process ratified that as as being the the kind of direction regular basis I do a roadmap priority review. I did one a few months ago knowing that we would we want to kind of hit the ground running after Saturn one. So, we do have a Saturn 2 that is ratified by governance, but there are two items that I think we're going to put Leaprog um into into the first. We might change things around. It might still be called Saturn 2, but Saturn 2 may end up being this. Um anyway, the what the road map looks like at the moment and it's still up in the air because um I need to uh go through and have this discussion with the with the community or at least I need to lock it down and that is there are two big um items. The first one is kind of like a minimum standard for node operators and that is using the execution layer exits to be able to exit validators if they go below below a certain threshold. Currently, we want to support home stakers as much as possible. We're quite lenient as a protocol in terms of performance, but we do need to set like a a bottom threshold to make sure that our eth um is as competitive as it it can be from a yield perspective. So, that's that's one that's one thing um that we're we're putting in place essentially to be able to eject code operators if they if they reach a certain floor. Then the other thing is redemptions. So for Ari um as I said before we've made the withdrawal liquidity much stronger with Saturn but we're going to build on top of that and build a redemption system. Um so if there is not enough withdrawal liquidity to allow instant um uh currently there's two ways. There's the withdrawal liquidity or the open market and both those provide instant staking. But if there's not enough withdrawal liquidity going forward, um we're going to provide a way to exit validators based on based on that demand. So that's still very much in design at the moment. We've had a few workshops, but we're we're looking at that. The main there's some couple of issues um a couple of things around that and that's mainly the withdrawal liquidity is really good and I think that covers off most of the kind of conservative staker I say conservative ster probably more pointing at institutions there. it covers off a lot of what they they need. Um and we're quite surprised actually that they don't need more. But that that actually um really work well the withdrawal liquidity can be quite deep. So that that makes it um really useful for them then. But with redemptions that is definitely something that as an ETF provider prov for example that it would be required for them. So that is we're working on those aspects to to be able to adopt that into that market. >> Got it. Yeah. super interesting upgrades still coming um in Saturn 2 then. And then uh finally interested in your thoughts about the role of um ETH staking and liquid staking and and just this kind of base um Ethereum staking yield in the kind of the the role of um of this stack in just like the more broader financial system. Now, as we're seeing this like convergence between crypto and DeFi and traditional finance, do you see that taking will will have more of this like differentiated investment appeal because it feels like crypto right now is just like lumped all together and kind of this risk asset. Going forward as the the space continues to mature, do you see it taking having a more differentiated role? Yeah, I think it's very tied obviously to ETH itself. As I said, I do think that I think there is more innovation out that we haven't really seen. We we've started to see, but particularly around some of the changes in the liquidity platforms, they've started to use liquid staking tokens to to innovate and to bolster the basically to drive liquidity demand, drive the liquidity, so through incentives and and that sort of thing. So there's definitely some innovation happening. I I think is a is an interesting primitive that I don't think we're really utilizing as best we can. do think later on as the things progress uh yeah I mean things like if you're depositing let's say for example you have even if it's a non-financial product right so say you're depositing funds into that into that product being able to take those funds and stake them and then you could potentially give your um your users free that could be like a new SAS model for example so being able to use that yield to pay um for the the software that you're um using, but you you can still use your funds within the within the protocol within the uh product itself. There are lots of different ways of of using um this as well as the the all the normal financial things that people are using it for as well. So, I do I do think there's still a lot of innovation once kind of crypto is everywhere or at least Ethereum is that um is that platform on which we can make value moves around. Yeah, there's there's a lot of innovation waiting to happen in that space. >> Yeah, I'm really really curious to see um what what happens when um crypto is more integrated into uh financial applications and just like the internet uh in general and I agree there there hasn't been that much innovation in this space. So yeah, we we'll have to uh see how uh this primitive is um is utilized in the future. But yeah, for now, congrats on Saturn 1. I think you know what what you guys are doing makes a lot of sense and excited to see Rocket Pool continue to grow uh and scale with these improvements. Thanks so much for uh walking me through everything that you guys are doing. >> Thank you very much. That's been fantastic. Thank you.
